title: List vs Tuple
code:
# Lists are mutable, tuples are not
lst = [1, 2, 3]
lst[0] = 10  # OK

t = (1, 2, 3)
# t[0] = 10  # TypeError!
note: Use tuples for fixed data — they're hashable and slightly faster.

title: Dictionary — O(1) lookup
code:
d = {'a': 1, 'b': 2}
print(d['a'])        # O(1) average
print('a' in d)     # O(1) average

# vs list lookup:
lst = ['a', 'b']
print('a' in lst)   # O(n)
note: Always prefer dict/set for membership checks over lists.

title: defaultdict — skip key checks
code:
from collections import defaultdict

graph = defaultdict(list)
graph['a'].append('b')  # No KeyError
graph['a'].append('c')

counts = defaultdict(int)
for ch in 'hello':
    counts[ch] += 1  # No need to check if key exists
note: Saves boilerplate if/else or .get() calls in graph and counting problems.

title: Counter — instant frequency map
code:
from collections import Counter

c = Counter('abracadabra')
print(c)                  # Counter({'a':5,'b':2,'r':2,'c':1,'d':1})
print(c.most_common(2))   # [('a',5),('b',2)]

# Counter arithmetic
c2 = Counter('abra')
print(c - c2)             # Counter({'a':2,'b':1,'c':1,'d':1,'r':1})
note: Use Counter for anagram checks, frequency problems, top-K.

title: deque — O(1) append/pop both ends
code:
from collections import deque

q = deque([1, 2, 3])
q.appendleft(0)  # O(1)
q.append(4)      # O(1)
q.popleft()      # O(1) — list.pop(0) is O(n)!
q.pop()          # O(1)
note: Essential for BFS and sliding window problems.

title: heapq — min-heap in Python
code:
import heapq

h = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5]
heapq.heapify(h)         # O(n)
heapq.heappush(h, 2)     # O(log n)
smallest = heapq.heappop(h)  # O(log n), returns 1

# Max-heap trick: negate values
max_h = []
heapq.heappush(max_h, -5)
heapq.heappush(max_h, -3)
largest = -heapq.heappop(max_h)  # 5
note: Python only has min-heap. Negate values for max-heap.

title: Set operations
note: Set ops are great for finding common elements, duplicates.

title: OrderedDict as LRU building block
note: move_to_end() and popitem(last=False) make LRU cache easy.

title: String slicing tricks
code:
s = 'hello world'
print(s[::-1])     # 'dlrow olleh' — reverse
print(s[::2])      # 'hlowrd' — every 2nd char
print(s[-5:])      # 'world' — last 5
print(s[:5])       # 'hello' — first 5
note: Slicing creates a new string. s[::-1] is the Pythonic way to reverse.

title: Useful string methods
code:
s = '  Hello, World!  '
s.strip()          # 'Hello, World!'
s.lower()          # '  hello, world!  '
s.split(', ')      # ['  Hello', 'World!  ']
'-'.join(['a','b'])  # 'a-b'
'abc'.isalnum()    # True
'123'.isdigit()    # True
'abc'.startswith('ab')  # True
note: These come up often in string manipulation problems.

title: String is immutable — use list for edits
code:
# Can't modify string in-place
s = 'hello'
# s[0] = 'H'  # TypeError!

# Convert to list, modify, join back
chars = list(s)
chars[0] = 'H'
s = ''.join(chars)  # 'Hello'
note: Building strings char-by-char? Append to list, then join — O(n) vs O(n^2).

title: f-strings and formatting
code:
name, age = 'Alice', 30
print(f'{name} is {age} years old')
print(f'{3.14159:.2f}')     # '3.14'
print(f'{42:08b}')          # '00101010' (binary)
print(f'{1000000:,}')       # '1,000,000'
note: f-strings are the modern Python way — faster than .format() or %.

title: List comprehension basics
code:
# Squares of even numbers
nums = range(10)
evens_sq = [x**2 for x in nums if x % 2 == 0]
# [0, 4, 16, 36, 64]

# Flatten 2D list
matrix = [[1,2],[3,4],[5,6]]
flat = [x for row in matrix for x in row]
# [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
note: Comprehensions are faster than equivalent for-loop + append.

title: Dict and set comprehensions
note: Same syntax as list comp but with {} or {k:v}.

title: Generator expressions — lazy evaluation
code:
# Generator — doesn't build full list in memory
total = sum(x**2 for x in range(1000000))

# vs list comp (allocates entire list first)
total = sum([x**2 for x in range(1000000)])

# Use generators for large data processing
def read_chunks(file, size=1024):
    while chunk := file.read(size):
        yield chunk
note: Generators use O(1) memory. Use for large sequences you iterate once.

title: sorted() with key
code:
# Sort by second element
pairs = [(1,3),(2,1),(3,2)]
sorted(pairs, key=lambda x: x[1])
# [(2,1),(3,2),(1,3)]

# Sort strings by length
words = ['banana','apple','fig']
sorted(words, key=len)  # ['fig','apple','banana']

# Sort descending
sorted([3,1,2], reverse=True)  # [3,2,1]
note: key= takes a function. Use lambda or operator.itemgetter.

title: enumerate — index + value
code:
fruits = ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']
for i, fruit in enumerate(fruits):
    print(f'{i}: {fruit}')
# 0: apple  1: banana  2: cherry

# Start from custom index
for i, fruit in enumerate(fruits, start=1):
    print(f'{i}: {fruit}')
note: Always prefer enumerate over range(len(lst)).

title: zip — iterate in parallel
code:
names = ['Alice', 'Bob']
scores = [95, 87]
for name, score in zip(names, scores):
    print(f'{name}: {score}')

# Unzip
pairs = [('a',1),('b',2),('c',3)]
letters, nums = zip(*pairs)
# letters=('a','b','c'), nums=(1,2,3)
note: zip stops at the shortest. Use itertools.zip_longest for unequal lengths.

title: any() and all()
code:
nums = [2, 4, 6, 8]
print(all(x % 2 == 0 for x in nums))  # True
print(any(x > 5 for x in nums))       # True

# Short-circuits — stops on first True/False
print(any(x > 100 for x in range(10**9)))  # eventually True
note: Use with generator expr for clean, readable condition checks.

title: map, filter, reduce
code:
# map — apply function to each element
list(map(str, [1,2,3]))  # ['1','2','3']
list(map(int, '123'))    # [1, 2, 3]

# filter
list(filter(lambda x: x>0, [-1,0,1,2]))  # [1,2]

# reduce — accumulate
from functools import reduce
reduce(lambda a,b: a*b, [1,2,3,4])  # 24
note: List comprehensions often preferred over map/filter for readability.

title: min/max with key
code:
words = ['banana', 'apple', 'fig', 'cherry']
print(min(words, key=len))  # 'fig'
print(max(words, key=len))  # 'banana'

# Find point closest to origin
points = [(3,4),(1,2),(5,0)]
closest = min(points, key=lambda p: p[0]**2+p[1]**2)
# (1, 2)
note: Same key= pattern as sorted(). Avoids writing manual loops.

title: Two pointers pattern
note: Use on sorted arrays. O(n) time, O(1) space.

title: Sliding window pattern
code:
def max_sum_subarray(nums, k):
    window = sum(nums[:k])
    best = window
    for i in range(k, len(nums)):
        window += nums[i] - nums[i-k]
        best = max(best, window)
    return best
note: Fixed window: expand right, shrink left. Variable: use while loop to shrink.

title: Binary search template
note: bisect module handles sorted list insertion/search.

title: BFS template
note: Use deque for O(1) popleft. Level-order: process len(queue) per loop.

title: DFS template (iterative + recursive)
note: Recursive is cleaner but can hit stack limit. Use iterative for deep graphs.

title: Backtracking template
note: Pattern: choose, explore, unchoose. Used in permutations, combinations, subsets.

title: Monotonic stack
note: Stack keeps decreasing sequence. Pop when you find something greater.

title: Prefix sum
code:
def prefix_sum(nums):
    prefix = [0] * (len(nums) + 1)
    for i in range(len(nums)):
        prefix[i+1] = prefix[i] + nums[i]
    return prefix

# Range sum query: sum(nums[l:r+1])
p = prefix_sum([1,2,3,4,5])
range_sum = p[4] - p[1]  # sum of index 1..3 = 2+3+4 = 9
note: Prefix[i] = sum of first i elements. Range sum in O(1).

title: HashMap for two sum pattern
note: Classic O(n) pattern: store complement in hashmap as you iterate.

title: Union-Find (Disjoint Set)
note: Path compression + union by rank = near O(1) per operation.

title: Mutable default arguments
code:
# BUG: default list is shared across calls!
def bad(items=[]):
    items.append(1)
    return items
bad()  # [1]
bad()  # [1, 1] — oops!

# FIX: use None as default
def good(items=None):
    if items is None:
        items = []
    items.append(1)
    return items
note: Default mutable args are created ONCE at function definition time.

title: Shallow vs deep copy
code:
import copy

a = [[1,2],[3,4]]
b = a[:]            # shallow copy
b = list(a)         # shallow copy
b = copy.copy(a)    # shallow copy
c = copy.deepcopy(a)  # deep copy

b[0][0] = 99
print(a[0][0])  # 99 — shallow copy shares inner lists!
print(c[0][0])  # 1  — deep copy is independent
note: Slicing and list() only copy the outer list. Nested objects are shared.

title: Integer caching (-5 to 256)
code:
a = 256
b = 256
print(a is b)  # True — cached

a = 257
b = 257
print(a is b)  # False (may vary)

# Always use == for value comparison
print(a == b)  # True
note: 'is' checks identity, '==' checks value. Always use '==' for comparisons.

title: Loop variable closure
code:
# BUG: all functions capture same variable i
funcs = []
for i in range(3):
    funcs.append(lambda: i)
print([f() for f in funcs])  # [2, 2, 2]

# FIX: capture with default arg
funcs = []
for i in range(3):
    funcs.append(lambda i=i: i)
print([f() for f in funcs])  # [0, 1, 2]
note: Lambda captures the variable, not its value. Use default arg to snapshot.

title: Modifying list while iterating
note: Never modify a list while iterating forward over it.

title: Common time complexities
code:
# O(1)   — dict/set lookup, append, pop
# O(log n) — binary search, heap push/pop
# O(n)   — linear scan, list.index()
# O(n log n) — sorting (Timsort)
# O(n^2) — nested loops, bubble sort
# O(2^n) — subsets, recursive fibonacci
# O(n!)  — permutations
note: Know these cold for interviews.

title: Python operation costs
code:
# LIST
# append: O(1) amortized
# pop():  O(1)     pop(0): O(n)!
# insert(i): O(n)  index(x): O(n)
# sort(): O(n log n)

# DICT / SET
# get/set/del/in: O(1) average
# iteration: O(n)

# STRING
# concatenation: O(n) each time!
# ''.join(list): O(n) total
note: Key insight: use deque for O(1) popleft, list for stack, dict for lookup.

title: Space complexity tips
code:
# In-place algorithms use O(1) extra space
# Sorting: Timsort uses O(n) space
# Recursion: O(depth) stack space
# BFS queue: O(width of graph)
# DFS stack: O(depth of graph)
# HashMap: O(n) space

# Trade-off: often space for time
# Memoization: O(n) space saves O(2^n) time
note: Interviewers love when you discuss space-time trade-offs.

title: itertools essentials
code:
import itertools

# All combinations of length 2
list(itertools.combinations([1,2,3], 2))
# [(1,2),(1,3),(2,3)]

# All permutations
list(itertools.permutations([1,2,3]))
# [(1,2,3),(1,3,2),(2,1,3),...]

# Cartesian product
list(itertools.product('ab', '12'))
# [('a','1'),('a','2'),('b','1'),('b','2')]

# Accumulate (prefix sums)
list(itertools.accumulate([1,2,3,4]))
# [1, 3, 6, 10]
note: itertools.accumulate is a clean alternative to manual prefix sums.

title: functools.lru_cache for memoization
note: Quick way to add memoization. Remember to use hashable args only.

title: Tuple unpacking and starred assignment
code:
# Basic unpacking
a, b, c = [1, 2, 3]

# Starred — capture the rest
first, *middle, last = [1,2,3,4,5]
# first=1, middle=[2,3,4], last=5

# Swap without temp
a, b = b, a

# Nested unpacking
(a, b), (c, d) = (1, 2), (3, 4)
note: Python's unpacking makes code cleaner than index-based access.

title: Infinity and float tricks
code:
# Infinity for comparisons
INF = float('inf')
min_val = float('inf')
max_val = float('-inf')

# Useful in DP and graph algorithms
dist = [float('inf')] * n
dist[start] = 0

# Integer limits (Python has arbitrary precision)
import sys
sys.maxsize  # 2^63-1 on 64-bit
note: float('inf') is cleaner than using sys.maxsize for min/max tracking.

title: ord() and chr() for character math
code:
# Convert char <-> ASCII number
ord('a')  # 97
chr(97)   # 'a'

# Character arithmetic
idx = ord('c') - ord('a')  # 2

# Build alphabet frequency array
freq = [0] * 26
for ch in 'hello':
    freq[ord(ch) - ord('a')] += 1
note: Use ord() for bucket-sort style character counting — O(1) space.

title: Walrus operator (:=) in comprehensions
code:
# Python 3.8+ — assign and use in one expression
# Filter and transform in one pass
results = [
    upper
    for word in ['hello', '', 'world', '']
    if (upper := word.upper())
]
# ['HELLO', 'WORLD']

# Read and process
import re
matches = [
    m.group()
    for line in lines
    if (m := re.search(r'\\d+', line))
]
note: Walrus := avoids computing an expression twice in filter+map combos.

title: Topological sort (Kahn's algorithm)
code:
from collections import deque, defaultdict

def topo_sort(n, edges):
    graph = defaultdict(list)
    indegree = [0] * n
    for u, v in edges:
        graph[u].append(v)
        indegree[v] += 1
    queue = deque(i for i in range(n) if indegree[i] == 0)
    order = []
    while queue:
        node = queue.popleft()
        order.append(node)
        for nei in graph[node]:
            indegree[nei] -= 1
            if indegree[nei] == 0:
                queue.append(nei)
    return order if len(order) == n else []  # empty = cycle
note: Used for course schedule, build order, dependency resolution.

title: Trie (Prefix Tree)
note: O(m) insert/search where m = word length. Great for autocomplete, word search.

title: Global vs local scope
code:
x = 10

def modify():
    # x += 1  # UnboundLocalError!
    # Need 'global' keyword:
    global x
    x += 1

# For nested functions, use 'nonlocal'
def outer():
    count = 0
    def inner():
        nonlocal count
        count += 1
    inner()
    return count  # 1
note: Use nonlocal in closures (common in DFS/backtracking helpers).

title: Sorting tricks for interviews
code:
# Sort by multiple keys
intervals = [(1,3),(2,1),(1,2)]
intervals.sort(key=lambda x: (x[0], x[1]))
# [(1,2),(1,3),(2,1)]

# Sort one key ascending, another descending
students = [('Alice',90),('Bob',90),('Charlie',85)]
students.sort(key=lambda x: (-x[1], x[0]))
# [('Alice',90),('Bob',90),('Charlie',85)]

# Python sort is stable!
# Equal elements keep their original order
note: Negate numeric keys for descending. Timsort is stable — exploit it.

title: collections.namedtuple
code:
from collections import namedtuple

Point = namedtuple('Point', ['x', 'y'])
p = Point(3, 4)
print(p.x, p.y)  # 3 4
print(p[0])       # 3 — still indexable

# Great for returning multiple values
def min_max(lst):
    Result = namedtuple('Result', ['min', 'max'])
    return Result(min(lst), max(lst))
r = min_max([3,1,4])
print(r.min, r.max)  # 1 4
note: More readable than plain tuples when fields have meaning.

title: divmod, pow, abs shortcuts
code:
# divmod — quotient and remainder at once
q, r = divmod(17, 5)  # q=3, r=2

# pow with modulus — for modular exponentiation
pow(2, 10, 1000)  # 1024 % 1000 = 24
# Much faster than (2**10) % 1000 for large numbers

# abs for distance
abs(-5)  # 5
abs(3+4j)  # 5.0 — works with complex!
note: pow(base, exp, mod) is O(log exp) — essential for modular math problems.

title: String matching with 'in' and find()
code:
s = 'hello world'

# Substring check — O(n)
'world' in s          # True
'xyz' not in s        # True

# Find index (returns -1 if not found)
s.find('world')       # 6
s.index('world')      # 6 (raises ValueError if not found)

# Count occurrences
s.count('l')          # 3

# Replace
s.replace('world', 'python')  # 'hello python'
note: Use 'in' for simple checks. For complex patterns, use re module.

title: == vs 'is' for None checks
code:
# Always use 'is' for None
x = None
if x is None:     # Correct
    pass
if x == None:     # Works but bad practice
    pass

# Because a class can override __eq__
class Weird:
    def __eq__(self, other):
        return True
w = Weird()
w == None   # True (misleading!)
w is None   # False (correct)
note: PEP 8: use 'is' / 'is not' for singleton comparisons like None.

title: Linked list — fast & slow pointers
note: Floyd's algorithm. Also used to find cycle start: reset one pointer to head.

title: Interval merge pattern
note: Sort by start time, then greedily merge overlapping intervals.

title: Matrix traversal helpers
note: Use this helper pattern for grid BFS/DFS problems.
